edges.description: Absorbed cadmium is transported to the liver for initial processing
Cadmium ions directly activate inflammatory signaling cascades
Circulating cadmium has direct toxic effects on bone cells
Cadmium causes oxidative stress in hepatocytes
Circulating cadmium disrupts vascular cholesterol homeostasis via miRNA modulation
Cd-MT complex released from liver is filtered by glomerulus and reabsorbed by proximal tubule
Accumulated free cadmium exceeds intracellular metallothionein binding capacity
Tubular cell injury and death cause loss of reabsorptive function
Sustained tubular injury leads to tubulointerstitial fibrosis
Impaired proximal tubular phosphate reabsorption causes phosphaturia
Chronic hypophosphataemia impairs hydroxyapatite deposition in bone matrix
Impaired osteoblast function compounds the mineralization defect from phosphate wasting
Oxidative stress from glutathione depletion triggers apoptotic cell death
Inflammatory cytokines exacerbate tubular cell damage
Inflammatory mediators compound hepatic oxidative stress

nodes.description: Cadmium enters the body via inhalation of fumes/dust or gastrointestinal absorption from contaminated food and water. Inhaled cadmium has 25-50% bioavailability; oral absorption is lower (3-8%) but enhanced by iron deficiency via shared divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1). Once absorbed, cadmium distributes via the bloodstream bound to albumin and accumulates in liver, kidney, and bone with a biological half-life of 10-30 years.

evidence.reference_title: Ailing bones and failing kidneys: a case of chronic cadmium toxicity.
evidence.snippet: Cadmium has a long biological half-life and there is no effective treatment for people who are exposed to it.

evidence.reference_title: An investigation and pathological analysis of two fatal cases of cadmium poisoning.
evidence.snippet: In both cases, multiple organ damage was observed, involving brain, lung, liver, kidney, red blood cells, and platelets

evidence.reference_title: Clinical characteristics, management, and outcomes of cadmium poisoning: a systematic review of case reports and case series.
evidence.snippet: The study also found that low iron stores exacerbate cadmium poisoning.

evidence.reference_title: Catch me if you can! Novel aspects of cadmium transport in mammalian cells.
evidence.snippet: uptake of free Cd(2+) has been demonstrated for the Fe(2+)/H(+) cotransporter divalent metal transporter 1.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium-binding proteins in human blood plasma.
evidence.snippet: this is the first study to reveal cadmium-binding proteins in real human blood plasma, which is extremely critical to our understanding of cadmium transportation and accumulation in human blood.

nodes.description: Cadmium disrupts cholesterol homeostasis in the vascular wall, promoting atherosclerosis through miRNA-mediated dysregulation of cholesterol uptake (CD36), efflux (ABCA1), and hydrolysis (NCEH1). Cadmium upregulates miR-30d-5p and downregulates miR-504-3p, promoting foam cell formation and intracellular lipid accumulation in macrophages. This pathway links cadmium exposure to increased cardiovascular risk, particularly ischemic stroke.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium Exposure Promotes Atherosclerosis by Disrupting Cholesterol Homeostasis via miR-30d-5p Regulation.
evidence.snippet: Cd exposure, even at a relatively low dosage (4 mg/L), significantly facilitates the progression of atherosclerosis in apolipoprotein E-deficient mice fed a high-fat diet. This pro-atherogenic effect was accompanied by comprehensive disturbances in systemic and vascular cholesterol homeostasis

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium Exposure Promotes Atherosclerosis by Disrupting Cholesterol Homeostasis via miR-30d-5p Regulation.
evidence.snippet: we identified miR-30d-5p and miR-504-3p as novel epigenetic regulators mediating Cd-induced foam cell formation. Specifically, Cd treatment upregulated miR-30d-5p and downregulated miR-504-3p, which directly targeted NCEH1 and CD36, respectively, thereby promoting intracellular lipid accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium Exposure Promotes Atherosclerosis by Disrupting Cholesterol Homeostasis via miR-30d-5p Regulation.
evidence.snippet: plasma miR-30d-5p levels were positively associated with Cd exposure and partially mediated the Cd-stroke association, accounting for 16.4% of the total effect.

nodes.description: Sustained proximal tubular injury from cadmium accumulation leads to tubulointerstitial inflammation, fibrosis, and progressive nephron loss. Glomerular filtration rate declines as tubulointerstitial nephritis advances, ultimately resulting in chronic kidney disease.

evidence.reference_title: A suspected case of "itai-itai disease" in a cadmium-polluted area in Akita prefecture, Japan.
evidence.snippet: She had chronic renal failure with a high Cd exposure level and advanced renal tubular dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Heavy metal poisoning: the effects of cadmium on the kidney.
evidence.snippet: Continued and heavy Cd exposure can progress to the clinical renal Fanconi syndrome, and ultimately to renal failure.

nodes.description: Chronic hypophosphataemia from renal phosphate wasting impairs hydroxyapatite crystal deposition in osteoid, causing osteomalacia. Bone becomes soft and prone to deformation and pathologic fractures. In itai-itai disease, severe demineralization causes fractures from minimal trauma, height loss, and skeletal deformities.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphataemic osteomalacia due to cadmium exposure in the silver industry.
evidence.snippet: Three silversmiths presented similarly with clinical, biochemical and radiological evidence of hypophosphataemic osteomalacia.

evidence.reference_title: A suspected case of "itai-itai disease" in a cadmium-polluted area in Akita prefecture, Japan.
evidence.snippet: The shortening of height, bone deformities and fractures, abnormal bone metabolism suggesting osteomalacia, and renal anemia were also noted.

nodes.description: Cadmium directly inhibits osteoblast differentiation and function independently of the renal phosphate wasting pathway. Cadmium disrupts calcium signaling in osteoblasts, inhibits alkaline phosphatase activity, and promotes osteoclast-mediated resorption, contributing to osteoporosis even before significant renal damage develops.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium exposure: health hazards of silver cottage industry in developing countries.
evidence.snippet: We will present a case of cadmium induced peripheral neuropathy, nephropathy, and decreased bone density.

nodes.description: Cadmium depletes hepatic glutathione stores and inhibits antioxidant enzymes (SOD, GSH-Px, CAT), disrupting the cellular redox balance. Lipid peroxidation increases (elevated MDA), and the glutathione metabolic pathway is overwhelmed. Cadmium also affects drug metabolism through altered cytochrome P450 activity.

evidence.reference_title: Proteomic analysis of toxic effects of short-term cadmium exposure on goat livers.
evidence.snippet: liver SOD, GSH-Px, T-AOC and CAT levels were decreased, and MDA level was increased in Cd-treated goats, and 630 DEPs (up 326, down 304) in the livers of Cd-treated goats.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise antagonizes cadmium-caused liver and intestinal injury in mice via Nrf2 and TLR2/NF-κB signalling pathway.
evidence.snippet: exercise significantly decreased blood ALT and AST levels, alleviating oxidative stress in the liver by reducing MDA synthesis and enhancing SOD and GSH-PX activities.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium exposure during adolescence and young adulthood induces signatures of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease.
evidence.snippet: Cd exposure altered hepatic lipid homeostasis via the perturbation of steatosis gene expression and lipid species abundances. Additionally, Cd exposure triggered a hepatic antioxidant response

nodes.description: The liver is the primary site of initial cadmium detoxification. Hepatocytes synthesize metallothionein (MT), a cysteine-rich protein that binds cadmium with high affinity. The cadmium-metallothionein (Cd-MT) complex is slowly released into the bloodstream over time. While MT binding initially protects against free cadmium toxicity, the Cd-MT complex is filtered at the glomerulus and taken up by renal tubular cells, effectively transferring the cadmium burden to the kidney.

evidence.reference_title: Renal cells exposed to cadmium in vitro and in vivo: normalizing gene expression data.
evidence.snippet: This half-life is partly as a result of metallothioneins (MTs), metal-binding proteins with a high affinity for Cd.

evidence.reference_title: Heavy metal poisoning: the effects of cadmium on the kidney.
evidence.snippet: The kidney is the main organ affected by chronic Cd exposure and toxicity. Cd accumulates in the kidney as a result of its preferential uptake by receptor-mediated endocytosis of freely filtered and metallothionein bound Cd (Cd-MT) in the renal proximal tubule.

nodes.description: Sustained oxidative stress from glutathione depletion triggers hepatocyte apoptosis via mitochondrial pathways. Cadmium causes release of pro- apoptotic proteins (cytochrome c, caspase-3, Bax) and nuclear damage, leading to progressive hepatocellular loss and liver injury.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise antagonizes cadmium-caused liver and intestinal injury in mice via Nrf2 and TLR2/NF-κB signalling pathway.
evidence.snippet: Exercise inhibited nuclear damage and hepatocyte apoptosis caused by Cd by increasing Bcl-2 protein expression and preventing the release of pro-apoptotic proteins such as caspase-3, Cytc, Bax, caspase-8and cleaved-caspase-3.

nodes.description: Injury to proximal tubular cells causes Fanconi syndrome, characterized by impaired reabsorption of low-molecular-weight proteins (beta-2- microglobulin, retinol-binding protein), glucose, amino acids, uric acid, and phosphate. This is the earliest and most sensitive clinical indicator of chronic cadmium nephrotoxicity, detectable before decline in GFR.

evidence.reference_title: Ailing bones and failing kidneys: a case of chronic cadmium toxicity.
evidence.snippet: We report the case of a 48-year-old man who presented with severe osteoporosis, impaired renal function and acquired Fanconi syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Cadmium impairs albumin reabsorption by down-regulating megalin and ClC5 channels in renal proximal tubule cells.
evidence.snippet: Cd reduced the transcriptional expression of megalin and ClC5 and, at the same time, increased the degradation of megalin and ClC5 proteins via the lysosomal pathway in an in vitro model of renal proximal tubular cells.

evidence.reference_title: In vitro Evaluation of The Effects of Cadmium on Endocytic Uptakes of Proteins into Cultured Proximal Tubule Epithelial Cells.
evidence.snippet: The exposure of S1 and S2 cells to Cd at 1 and 3 µM for 3 days resulted in significant decreases in the uptakes of β2-MG and metallothionein but not in those of albumin or transferrin.

evidence.reference_title: Heavy metal poisoning: the effects of cadmium on the kidney.
evidence.snippet: An early and sensitive manifestation of chronic Cd renal toxicity, which can be useful in individual and population screening, is impaired reabsorption of low molecular weight proteins (LMWP)

nodes.description: Cadmium activates pro-inflammatory signaling cascades including the NF-kB and MAPK/JNK pathways, leading to increased secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1beta, IL-6, TNF-alpha, IL-8, CCL2) and upregulation of COX-2. This chronic inflammatory state exacerbates organ-specific injury in kidney, liver, and intestine.

evidence.reference_title: Andrographolide Reduces Cytokine Release and Cyclooxygenase-2 Expression by Inhibiting the JNK and NF-κB Pathways in Glioblastoma Cells Exposed to Cadmium.
evidence.snippet: the environmental pollutant cadmium is known to increase the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including interleukin (IL)-6, IL-8, and chemokine (C-C motif) ligand 2 (CCL2) by activating the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Exercise antagonizes cadmium-caused liver and intestinal injury in mice via Nrf2 and TLR2/NF-κB signalling pathway.
evidence.snippet: exercise, both before and during Cd exposure, can reduce Cd caused pathological damages in the liver and duodenum of mice, suppressing the expression levels of the IL-1β, IL-6 and TNF-α genes.

nodes.description: When intracellular cadmium exceeds the metallothionein binding capacity of proximal tubular cells (typically at renal cortex concentrations above 200 mcg/g), free cadmium ions cause oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and activation of apoptotic pathways. Cadmium displaces zinc from zinc-finger proteins and disrupts calcium signaling, leading to tubular cell death.

evidence.reference_title: A suspected case of "itai-itai disease" in a cadmium-polluted area in Akita prefecture, Japan.
evidence.snippet: She had chronic renal failure with a high Cd exposure level and advanced renal tubular dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Heavy metal poisoning: the effects of cadmium on the kidney.
evidence.snippet: Internalised Cd-MT is degraded in endosomes and lysosomes, releasing free Cd(2+) into the cytosol, where it can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and activate cell death pathways.

nodes.description: Impaired proximal tubular phosphate reabsorption leads to chronic phosphaturia and hypophosphataemia. The sustained phosphate loss is the primary metabolic driver of cadmium-induced osteomalacia, as phosphate is essential for hydroxyapatite crystal formation in bone.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphataemic osteomalacia due to cadmium exposure in the silver industry.
evidence.snippet: Three silversmiths presented similarly with clinical, biochemical and radiological evidence of hypophosphataemic osteomalacia.

nodes.description: The cadmium-metallothionein (Cd-MT) complex is freely filtered at the glomerulus due to its small molecular weight (~7 kDa). Proximal tubular epithelial cells reabsorb Cd-MT via receptor-mediated endocytosis through the megalin/cubilin receptor complex. Once internalized, Cd-MT is degraded in lysosomes, releasing free cadmium ions intracellularly. This mechanism explains the kidney's particular vulnerability to cadmium accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: Catch me if you can! Novel aspects of cadmium transport in mammalian cells.
evidence.snippet: the multiligand endocytic receptors megalin and cubilin take up cadmium-metallothionein complexes via receptor-mediated endocytosis.

evidence.reference_title: Increased Endocytosis of Cadmium-Metallothionein through the 24p3 Receptor in an In Vivo Model with Reduced Proximal Tubular Activity.
evidence.snippet: Cd2+ complexed to metallothionein (MT) (CdMT) is taken up through receptor-mediated endocytosis (RME) via the PT receptor megalin:cubilin, which is the predominant pathway for reuptake of filtered proteins in the kidney.

evidence.reference_title: Heavy metal poisoning: the effects of cadmium on the kidney.
evidence.snippet: Cd accumulates in the kidney as a result of its preferential uptake by receptor-mediated endocytosis of freely filtered and metallothionein bound Cd (Cd-MT) in the renal proximal tubule. Internalised Cd-MT is degraded in endosomes and lysosomes, releasing free Cd(2+) into the cytosol

evidence.reference_title: Renal cells exposed to cadmium in vitro and in vivo: normalizing gene expression data.
evidence.snippet: The high retention properties of the kidneys reside in proximal tubular cells that possess transport mechanisms for Cd-MT uptake, ultimately leading to more Cd accumulation.
